name: Floriston (Bronco Site)
description: Established in 1872 as Bronco, this railroad wood stop became a noted ice and lumber center and was renamed in 1891. The Floriston Pulp and Paper Company built the present town after 1899 and operated one of the worldÃ
address: Eastbound Floriston offramp on I-80 east of Truckee

image.alt: Floriston (Bronco Site), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Foote's Crossing Road
description: Foote's Crossing Road was built by A. D. Foote in 1913 as an all-season short-cut from Columbia Hill to the mines of the Alleghany area. Foote was president of the Tightner Mine Co., the largest gold producer in the region at that time. The road, a 15-foot shelf hanging on the sheer canyon wall above the Middle Fork of the Yuba River, is a spectacular engineering feat and scenic wonder. The rock walls, built without mortar are still intact today. Unforeseen difficulties in building the road caused it to far exceed specifications so Foote personally financed 60% of the total cost, the balance being paid by Nevada and Sierra counties and the U.S. Forest Service.
address: From intersection of Cruzon Grade and Tyler-Foote Rds in Nevada County to Alleghany in Sierra County (See Note on access below)

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Foote's Crossing Road
image.originalAlt: Foote's Crossing Road in

name: Gaston
description: A historic mining community. Placer mining around Gaston began in the early 1850's. The first hard rock mine, the California Mine, was established in 1856. An ample supply of water was ensured by the arrival of the North Bloomfield ditch in 1870. In 1898, the mine, now known as the Gaston Mine, was acquired by the San Francisco based Gaston Gold Mining Company, which substantially upgraded operations. A town blossomed around the mine. The population was recorded as about 200 in 1900. There was stage service to the town of Washington, and to Nevada City. By 1904, the town boasted two stores, a hotel, a saloon, a hardware store, a Post Office, a water system, electric lights, a telephone line to Nevada City, a fire company and a school. The mine closed during World War I, because of a shortage of labor and supplies needed for the war effort. There was intermittent mining after the war. By the 1940s', the last remaining building was in shambles. Today, nothing readily visible remains of Gaston.
address: Roughly midway between Graniteville and town of Washington, near the intersection of Gaston and Hoosier Rds, in the Tahoe National Forest.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Gaston
image.originalAlt: Gaston in

name: Gateway Cabin
description: This cabin was one of several built by Jack Wolert for Dick Joseph in 1939 on US Route 40. The cabin was constructed of lumber salvaged from houses being dismantled five miles north of Truckee at Hobart Mills and served as the Gateway Motel's office. Originally located at the site of the current Gateway Shopping Center on Donner Pass Road (historic US 40). It was donated to the Truckee Donner Historical Society in 1990 and moved to this location on May 23, 1991. Thanks to community efforts, the cabin has been restored and serves as a historical research library.

image.alt: Gateway Cabin, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: George Schaffer
description: One of Truckee's earliest settlers, Schaffer in 1866 built the first lumber mill in the town. He freighted the locomotive 'San Mateo' to Truckee in a winter crossing of the Sierra prior to the laying of Central Pacific tracks. This building 'The Star Hotel' was built by Schaffer probably for his family. When mill operations were relocated up Martis Creek in 1871, this building was converted to a hotel and has remained in continuous operation to this day. For 24 years, the Truckee Basin supplied timber to railroads, mines and boom towns through 'Uncle George's' enterprises. Note: The Schaffer family cemetery, located at 10233 Schaffer Dr., may be visited by the public.

image.alt: George Schaffer, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Gilmore Flying Field
description: Gilmore Flying Field. This windsock marks the site of one of the first flying fields in the United States. Here Lyman Gilmore, a contemporary of the Wright Brothers, pioneered in aviation by designing and building his own planes and gliders as early as 1898. Plaque on monument in front of school dedicated 1968 by Grand Lodge, Free and Accepted Masons: Lyman Gilmore, Jr. 1874-1951. Lyman Gilmore, Jr. established the first commercial airport on this site in 1907. To him and his memory the people of this community dedicate this school as the Lyman Gilmore School. Note: Gilmore did not use this field until the winter of 1911-12. As the Daily Transcript reported on January 29, 1912, "The principal place of business [of the Gilmore Airship Co.] was recently moved from Colfax to Grass Valley, and the meeting on Saturday evening was the first with the present board of directors." This pole was used originally at Gilmore Field, which Gilmore always claimed was the first commercial airport on the West Coast. The CETA mural on the north wall of the multipurpose building was painted in 1978.
address: Lyman Gilmore School, corner Gilmore Way and W. Main St, Grass Valley

image.alt: Gilmore Flying Field, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Glasson House
description: This Victorian/Greek Revival house was built in 1866 for Miles P. O'Connor, who came to California in 1849 and to Nevada County in 1852. He served as justice of the peace for Little York township in 1852-53 and for Grass Valley 1862-69. He served one term in the state assembly and two in the state senate. He earned a fortune from the famous Idaho gold mine and was instrumental in creation of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. After selling this house to John Polglase in 1873, he moved to San Jose and funded various institutions, including a sanitarium later known as O'Connor Hospital. John Polglase, a hard-rock miner from Cornwall, came to Grass Valley in 1864. In 1875 Polglase bought an interest in John Glasson's grocery and provisions store while continuing to work underground in the Idaho mine. In 1882 he sold the house to his partner, and Glasson and his heirs owned it from then until 1973. John Glasson, like Polglase, came here in 1864 from England, and like O'Connor he played a prominent role in Nevada County history. He bought the local gas company in 1885-86 and in 1888 merged it with the Grass Valley Electric Light and Motor Co. He built the countyÃ

image.alt: Glasson House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Glenbrook Basin
description: Early In the 1850's Glenbrook Basin first became known as the site of county fairs, horse racing and other special events. When Olympia Park, located at the south end of the basin, opened in 1901, Glenbrook Basin became the recreational center of Nevada County and much of Northern California, and remained so for over fifty years. People would come to the basin by horse and buggy or later the automobile, but those wishing for a little more excitement would often use the Nevada County Traction Company's trolley, or the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. The large grandstands of the three-quarter-mile racetrack stood near the junction of present Brunswick Road and Sutton Way. Trotting, pacing, and running horse races were held at the track, and motor car races began in 1913. The racetrack was one of the first electrically lighted racetracks in the state. A dam was constructed just northeast of the existing electrical substation to form Lake Olympia. The lake soon became one of the BasinÃ
address: Sutton Way, south of Brunswick Rd, next to bus shelter in Glenbrook Plaza parking lot

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Glenbrook Basin
image.originalAlt: Glenbrook Basin in

name: Graniteville
description: A camp established in this area in 1850 was called Eureka; then later, Eureka South. By 1856 it sometimes had a winter population of 1000. Rich placer mining deposits were played out by 1865, but quartz mining continued. By 1867, total gold taken from this area was estimated at 20 million dollars. Area also important as distribution point for water from reservoirs, fed to hydraulic mines below by ditches. Name changed to Graniteville when Post Office was established on Aug. 26, 1867.
address: No. Bloomfield-Graniteville Rd, across from house at No. 35177. 50 ft past gazebo bearing Graniteville sign.

image.alt: Graniteville, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Grass Valley AME Church and School Site
description: "Across the street, on lots 315, 317, 319 and 323 S. Church Street, the Grass Valley African Methodist Episcopal Church and School were erected in 1854 and 1865 respectively by the local African American community. Reverend Peter Green and Church trustee and former slave Isaac Sanks, along with the other members of the congregation, worked to abolish slavery, secure voting rights for African American men, eliminate the ban on testimony of African Americans in court, and gain access to public accommodations and education. The AME Church was known for its exceptional choir and accomplished musicians. It was the site for fundraising events, literary readings, and the celebration of the Emancipation Proclamation. African American parents, whose children were denied entry into white schools, built their own schoolhouse next to the Church. Both the Church and School were sold in 1893 at public auction to Charles Clinch and the funds returned to the AME Church. Shortly thereafter, the new owners built the four Victorian homes that still stand on the property today."
address: 315-323 S. Church Street, Grass Valley. Plaque located across the street in front of 328 S. Church St.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Grass Valley AME Church and School Site

name: Hard Rock Gold Mining
description: Hard Rock Gold Mining 1850-1957. Within a one-mile radius of this spot more than 500 million dollars worth of gold was produced by deep mines, including the Empire, North Star and the Idaho-Maryland. During that time this was the worldÃ
address: Memorial Park, Grass Valley, near creek by Race St culvert

image.alt: Hard Rock Gold Mining, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Joseph Nguyen, G Tour Photos, via Google

name: Hartung House
description: In August of 1888 Nevada and Emma (Schwartz) Hartung purchased about 220 acres bordering Pleasant Valley Rd. and Piper Lane in Pleasant Valley (now Penn Valley). At the time of purchase, a small house and a barn were on the property. These two structures likely were built in the early 1860s when the property was owned by James and Hannah Saxon. The first house, still standing in the 1930's, had deteriorated and was razed. The well, located in the front porch of the house remains. The barn now extended on both ends, still exists. The Hartungs built the current house in 1889. Pear, apple and walnut crops became the primary means of support for the family. A few of the original pear trees remain. Nevada August Hartung was born in August 1853 on the shores of Webber Lake, California in a wagon train on the family's way west from Illinois. It is reported that Nevada's given name was the result of the parents' awe at their first sight of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. He grew up on his father Adolph Hartung's farm at Kentucky Flat and married Emma Schwartz in 1880. By the time of their 1888 purchase of the property, the Hartungs had three children, Frank (b.1880), Herman (b. 1884) and Mamie (b. 1886). The Hartungs bought the farm from Samuel and Agnes Yeo (owners 1888-1888) and prior to that the property was owned by Joseph Peers (1886-1888) and the Saxons (ca.1860-1886). In 1849-1850, John Rose and William Reynolds operated a trading post and cattle corral, both thought to be located on what became the Hartung property. The current owner, Pete Brennan, acquired the property in 1971 and operates a horse boarding facility.

image.alt: Hartung House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Hobart Mills Site
description: First plaque dedicated 1938 by descendants of Walter Scott Hobart is about 0.2 m. north of the junction of Hobart Mills Rd with Old Hwy 89 on west side of Old 89: This monument marks the townsite of Hobart Mills, California. A logging and lumbering enterprise of sixty-six years life in the Sierra Nevada mountains it commemorates and honors its founder Walter Scott Hobart 1839-1892 and those whose loyalty, industry and ability made its successful operation possible.
address: Just east of Hwy 89, five miles north of Truckee. There are two plaques, about 200 yards apart

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Hobart Mills Site
image.originalAlt: Hobart Mills Site in

name: Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque
description: Walter Scott Hobart's Sierra Nevada Wood & Lumber Company moved machinery, buildings and railroad equipment here from Lake Tahoe in 1896. The town and business flourished for 40 years, ceasing operations in 1936. Fibreboard Corporation operated a mill and railroad here from 1946 to 1955, logging timber purchased from Southern Pacific. The old mill was burned in 1964. Hobart Mills was an important lumbering center and a major contributor to the heritage of the Eastern Sierra.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque
image.originalAlt: Hobart Mills Site Second Plaque in

name: Hooligan Rock
description: Behind this broken rock, vigilante justice in the form of a tar and feather party or other form of physical punishment was meted out to the villains and ruffians who plagued Truckee through the dawn of the 20th-century. Constable Jacob Teeter bravely fought against vigilantism and his friend, attorney C. F. McGlashan, blasted the rock, ending its symbolism of terror, and used parts of the rock in a wall around his mansion. His granddaughter, Nona, recalled that the rock formed a 'backdrop of ugly secret scenes, floggings in the night, scalding tar and a smother of feathers, curses and screams for mercy.
address: 11200 Donner Pass Rd, east end of parking lot

image.alt: Hooligan Rock, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Gold Crush Climbing Gym, via Google

name: Humbug/North Blomfield Cemetery
description: "This 'hallowed place', that resides with in the boundaries of the Malakoff State Historical Park represents a 'tapestry of humanity.' Those who dared to carve out a place for themselves in an unknown and untamed wilderness. They came seeking riches, changing the landscape they encountered for all of time. They left behind a legacy entwined with turmoil, destruction, invention and lives lived hard. In 1855, Isreal Joiner, who was shot for stealing gold from a 'sluice box', and George Carter who lived to be 80, were the first to be buried in Humbug Cemetery. In 1857 when the town got its post office, the name was changed to North Bloomfield. George Edwards, John Enders, John McNulty, and Louise Miller; they were the first to be buried under the new banner, North Bloomfield Cemetery. By the end of the Nineteenth Century, as the mining played out, this town was all but dead. However, in an almost bazaar [sic] manner this cemetery has continued to grow! As of 1984 there were 170 recorded buried here from Austria, Australia, Bavaria, Canada, China, Denmark, England, France, Germany, Holland, Ireland, Portugal, Prussia, Scotland and America. In March 2017, Debra Phanner, a long time Park asst. was buried here. So, as you enter this portal into the past know that you are walking on 'hallowed ground' with many untold stories waiting to be discovered!"
address: North Bloomfield Rd, west of North Bloomfield, Malakoff Diggins State Historical Park

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Humbug/North Blomfield Cemetery

name: Hunt's Hill (Gouge Eye)
description: Hunt's Hill (also Gouge Eye) is a former mining camp founded in 1852 by a miner named Hunt. It was located on one of the deepest parts of the rich Blue Lead channel of gold-bearing gravel. In 1855, one of the mining claims established by some French miners, was "jumped.â€
address: About 6 m. east of Nevada City, off Buckeye Rd, about 1 m. from the intersection with Red Dog Rd

image.alt: Hunt's Hill (Gouge Eye), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Hydraulic Gate Valve
description: Hydraulic gold mining 21-inch gate valve 1880 Used at the You Bet-Red Dog Diggin's east of Nevada City periodically 1880- 1935 to control water flow from Chalk Bluff reservoir to the mine. Hydraulic nozzle mounted opposite was operated at that mine by Thomas B. Brady. Manufactured by Joshua Handy Works, San Francisco. Wt. 1,500 lbs. approx. A gift from the Brady Family.

name: Jacob van Blaren
description: Anthony House Way Station, Pub & Tavern was located just off shore from Commodore Park. It was a stage stop between Marysville & the Virginia City Silver Mines. Jacob Van Blaren, a Civil War bugler, stopped at Anthony House for a period of time and died while staying there. He was in his forties at his death. Proprietors of Anthony House decided to bury him behind the building and it wasn't until Boise Cascade was in negotiations with the historical society that they named the area surrounding his burial site Bugler Park. It is uncertain at what point in Lake Wildwood's history that the park's name was changed to Commodore Park
address: Just east of 19321 Wildflower Dr, Lake Wildwood

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Jacob van Blaren

name: Jax Truckee Diner
description: Originally built in October 1948 by Kullman Diner Co. Newark, New Jersey and shipped to West Chester, PA. known as the “Birmingham Grill” where it continually operated until purchased by the Carey Family in 1992. On July 26, 1995 the diner arrived in Truckee, California. No other diner of it's (sic) size and class had ever been trucked across the continent. After extensive restoration the diner was re-opened to the public on December 7, 1995.
address: Corner of Bridge and W. River Sts.,

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Jax Truckee Diner

name: Jibboom Street
description: Wooden plaque dedicated and placed in Pastime Club Bar 1969. Bronze plaque rededicated and placed in present location 1979 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus: Jibboom Street. Sierra Site of the World's Oldest Profession 1867-1949.The stone and bronze plaque have been removed and are awaiting rededication
address: Southwest corner Jibboom and Bridge Sts

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Jibboom Street

name: Kohler Building
description: Built by Henry Kohler, pioneer merchant and miner born in Germany in 1825. He came to Washington from Indiana in 1854. He mined until 1861 when he opened a general merchandise store to supply groceries, hardware and mining supplies to the area.
address: Washington Rd, next to hotel in town of Washington

image.alt: Kohler Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Kruger-White House
description: Designated a National Historic Landmark 1982: This property Kruger-White House has been placed on the National Register of Historic Places by the United States Department of the Interior. Note: W. H. Kruger, a lumberman and entrepreneur, built this impressive home 107 years ago. Mr. Kruger contributed significantly to the 19th century community development of Truckee as one of the town's leading commercial pioneers. Mr. C. B. White, a banker, bought the house in 1904. Today, the Kruger-White home stands as an historic architectural landmark in a town which, for over a century, has represented an important gateway to California.

image.alt: Kruger-White House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: La Grange Giant
description: This monitor was used at the now closed La Grange mine near Weaverville in Trinity County and is similar in size to those used at the Malakoff to wash down the gravel hillsides. Note: Historians still disagree over which mine was larger; the La Grange or the North Bloomfield.
address: Location: Next to museum in N. Bloomfield

image.alt: La Grange Giant, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company Site
description: The Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company was incorporated on December 1898 by Duane Leroy Bliss. The Narrow Gauge Railroad opened for seasonal traffic on May 1, 1900. The line ran from the Southern Pacific Depot in Truckee, 15 miles south to the shores of Lake Tahoe. Tahoe City hosted the railroad's shops. The Tahoe Tavern hotel and pier where the train met the Bliss steamer - Tahoe. Southern Pacific Purchased the line and converted it to standard gauge in 1926. Passenger Service operated until abandonment in November 1943.
address: Just east of 10130 W. River St, across the railroad tracks from Amtrak station. The plaque is missing. If you have any information about its whereabouts, please contact the publisher.

image.alt: Lake Tahoe Railway & Transportation Company Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

description: Plaque dedicated 2003 by Marine Corps League Gold Country Detachment 885: "These memorials honor those who served in the Armed Forces of the United States of America. For those who returned, thank you God. For those who died, glory forever God. For those still missing, please God."

name: McGlashan Point
description: Dedicated August 10,1986 in honor of Charles Fayette McGlashan 1847-1931. Truckee's Patriarch, Historian, Author, Editor, Attorney, Legislator, Inventor, Entomologist and Astronomer. His last public address was given in 1926 at the Donner Summit Bridge Dedication. This site is named in his memory by the people of Truckee.
address: Overlook next to Donner Summit Bridge on Donner Pass Rd, Old Hwy 40

image.alt: McGlashan Point, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: McGuashen Butterfly Collection *
description: "Charles Fayette McGlashan, noted author of History of the Donner Party: A Tragedy of the Sierra, started his butterfly collection in 1872. In the early 1900s, his daughter, Ximena, joined him in collecting and arranging the Lepidoptera collection. Ximena, then referred to as The Butterfly Princess, was instrumental in growing the collection and started her own business, The Butterfly Farm. She continued this venture until she attended college. With her father, she also wrote and published The Butterfly Farmer, a monthly periodical. Their collection included up to 20,000 specimens, and they were granted a number of entomological mounting patents. First housed in the original Rocking Stone Tower, the collection was moved several times and finally moved here in 2015."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for McGuashen Butterfly Collection *

name: Memorial Arch
description: First plaque: "THE ARCH Erected on the entrance of the old Masonic building in 1909. Destroyed by a gas explosion in 1993. Dedicated to the Town of Truckee - October 27, 2007 by Truckee Lodge #200 F&AM Gary E. Miner, W.M. Dedicated to Truth, Equality & Freedom." Second plaque: "Celebrating 150 years of Freemasons in Truckee. Lodge #200 established on October 14, 1869. Dedicated to the Town of Truckee October 14, 2019 by Truckee Lodge #200 F&AM Christopher Rochefort, W.M. Dedicated to Truth, Equality & Freedom." Note: The Arch is all that remains of the historic Masonic Lodge # 200 building, built in 1909 and destroyed by fire and explosion in 1993. It reportedly was the only lodge to have had a chiseled, Sierra granite entrance-way, which was preserved after the fire. In 2007, the entrance-way Arch was reconstructed and placed on the grounds of the Truckee-Donner Historical Society's Old Jail Museum.
address: Behind the Old Jail, northeast corner of Spring and Jibboom Sts

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Memorial Arch

name: Memorial Park GV
description: Memorial Park was built in 1921 in memory of local armed forces members who died in World War I. The land and swimming pool were donated by the Empire Mine Co.; the club house was donated by the Idaho Maryland Mine Co.; and people of the area subscribed $8,000. It now contains five plaques honoring those who served our country in the armed forces.
address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley

image.alt: Memorial Park GV, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Mooney Flat Hotel
description: Mooney Flat Hotel 1856-1958. Site of the first 3-story team & stage stop on the Henness Pass route to the Northern Mines and Comstock Lode. Built and operated by George and Veronica Schmidt. Served miners, travelers, and teamsters.
address: In front of private home at 10885 Mooney Flat Rd, one mile north of Hwy 20

image.alt: Mooney Flat Hotel, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Mt. Elisha Stephens
description: Stephens-Townsend-Murphy Party of 1844 First Pioneer Wagons Over the Sierra Nevada. On May 22, 1844, this small wagon party of 50 men, women and children 'jumped off' from Council Bluffs, Iowa, bound for California. These courageous pioneers were the first emigrants to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The Murphy, Townsend, Miller, and Hitchcock families formed the main groups of the party. Caleb Greenwood was the hired guide, and Elisha Stephens was elected Captain of the wagon train. After following the Oregon Trail to Fort Hall, Idaho, they turned away from the well-worn path and headed west into the unfamiliar Nevada desert. A Paiute Indian Chief showed them how to reach a river flowing from the Sierra Nevada, which they named 'Truckee' in his honor. Overcoming severe conditions, they followed the Truckee River to the base of the Sierra. Six members, on horseback, continued to follow the Truckee River and eventually arrived safely at Sutter's New Helvetia on December 10, 1844. Six of the wagons were left in the charge of two men, and young Moses Schallenberger, near Truckee Lake (present Donner Lake). Near this spot, with two feet of snow on the ground, they worked the remaining five wagons across the summit of the mighty Sierra Nevada on November 25, 1844. Deepening snow forced them to camp near Big Bend, on the South Yuba River. Leaving the wagons with two men and the women and children, the remaining 17 men continued on to New Helvetia to seek help. Somehow, John Sutter persuaded the men to enlist in his forces taking part in a Mexican civil war. Eventually the families were reunited, and all members (including two babies born on the way) arrived safely at Sutter's Fort in early March 1845. Note: Mount Elisha Stephens is west of bridge on north side of road.
address: Parking lot next to Donner Summit Bridge on Donner Pass Rd, Old Hwy 40

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Mt. Elisha Stephens
image.originalAlt: Mt. Elisha Stephens in

name: Mt. Saint Mary's Convent and Academy
description: Mt. St. Mary's School. To this spot Father Thomas J. Dalton brought the Sisters of Mercy in 1863. The Sisters of Mercy have taught here since that date. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1972 Built by the Reverend Thomas J. Dalton and dedicated May 2, 1865 by Bishop Eugene O'Connell as Sacred Heart Convent and Holy Angels Orphanage. Under the Sisters of Mercy it served from 1866 to 1932 as the first orphanage of the Northern Mines. It functioned as an academy (1868-1965) and a convent (1866-1968).

image.alt: Mt. Saint Mary's Convent and Academy, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Murphy Cabin
description: The face of this rock formed the north end and the fireplace of the Murphy Cabin. General Stephen W. Kearney, on June 22, 1847, buried under the middle of the cabin the bodies found in the vicinity. Following is a complete list of members of the Donner party who occupied the various cabins and tents.This and the other Donner campsites were designated a National Historical Landmark in 1961. Placed on the National Register.

description: Nevada City, the 'Queen City of the Northern Mines', became a town of 10,000 to 16,000 in the early 1850s following the discovery of gold on Deer Creek in 1849. Here is located the largest and best preserved historical downtown district in California Gold Country. As the county seat of Nevada County, Nevada City has maintained its position as the countyÃ

description: Landmark registered 1972 by State of California, CHL 852. Plaque dedicated 1972 by State in cooperation with Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus and Malakoff Citizens Advisory Committee: This was a major hydraulic operation in California. Its vast system of canals and flumes using a drainage tunnel 7800 feet long was termed a feat of engineering skill. It was the principal defendant in an anti-debris lawsuit settled by the Sawyer decision in 1884. SawyerÃ

description: North Bloomfield School 1872-73. Built at a cost of some 3,000 dollars. Modeled after schoolhouses at nearby Sebastopol and Sweetland, it served the community until closed in 1941. Acquired from Washington School District, Nevada County, for public use and display. Restoration commenced in 1973 by popular subscription.

name: North San Juan
description: North San Juan 1853. Dedicated to the memory of the founder Christian Keinz and to all the pioneer argonauts of San Juan Ridge, who by their labor and fortitude contributed so greatly to the wealth and economy of Nevada County, California and the nation.
address: In North San Juan on front of Bigley's Market, Hwy 49

name: North San Juan Methodist Episcopal Church
description: Erected in 1857 at a cost of $1500, the structure Is the earliest existing church in the county. It contains early furnishings. Second plaque dedicated 1980 by the church board and Chap 10 E Clampus Vitus: North San Juan United Methodist Church 1856. One of the oldest of its faith in continuous operation in California. Escaped three major fires which destroyed the town. The Rev. H. B. Sheldon, first assigned pastor, rode circuit from Brandy City to French Corral on horseback.
address: End of Flume St off Hwy 49

image.alt: North San Juan Methodist Episcopal Church, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: North San Juan Protestant Cemetery
description: The North San Juan Protestant Cemetery is an unusually well preserved and beautiful cemetery and has served as a place of interment since 1851. It holds the remains of hundreds of individuals, with many life stories related to the Gold Rush. The grave monuments within its boundary are exceptionally varied in design and represent fine examples of gravestone art and symbolism. Situated in a lovely tree covered setting, it is one of the best examples of nineteenth century burial customs and history.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for North San Juan Protestant Cemetery

name: Old Auburn Road Bridge (Site)
description: The Old Auburn Bridge was registered as a landmark in 1996 by Nevada County. The bridge consisted of a single span, pin-connected steel pony truss, 66-feet in length, 11-feet in width, with a timber deck carrying a single traffic lane. It was built in 1897 by Cotton Brothers and Co. of Oakland, California. In 2004, the old bridge was dismantled by the County as unsafe and replaced by a modern span. Recognizing the historical significance of the spot, the County erected a monument containing a bronze plaque: Historic Marker Perrin Crossing. This bridge replaces the original steel truss span constructed in 1897 and remained in continuous service until 2004. This crossing received its name from Joseph and Susan Perrin, who settled nearby in 1851. Close to this crossing the Perrin family operated a stagecoach stop known for its hospitality. This road was part of the original stagecoach route between Sacramento and Grass Valley. Note: Remains of the stagecoach stop can be seen about 100 ft. south of the monument
address: Old Auburn Rd, southeast of modern bridge over Wolf Creek about 4 m. south of McCourtney Rd

image.alt: Old Auburn Road Bridge (Site), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old Bear River Bridge
description: Since 1852 bridges near this site have served as the gateway to western Nevada County. This 1924 span was the first concrete bridge at this site and features a Roman style single arch design, constructed in a continuous pour method. The Colfax Highway Association led a citizens group that saved the bridge from the wrecker's ball in 1986, and placed this monument in 1992.
address: Hwy 174 at Bear River, between Colfax and Chicago Park

image.alt: Old Bear River Bridge, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old Capitol Building
description: The Old Capitol. Erected in 1870. In its heyday it was the social center of Truckee . . . Many of the leading thespians . . . politicians . . . and other celebrities of that era gathered here . . . Note: This plaque has disappeared. A plaque placed on the front of building by unknown persons reads: Capitol Building 1870 Truckee California. The first brick building in Truckee, it was originally Hurd's Saloon, then the Capitol Saloon, later used by the Piper Opera Company and the County Court.

image.alt: Old Capitol Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old Chinese Herb Shop
description: Old Chinatown. Construction of the Central Pacific Railroad over Donner Summit brought thousands of Chinese laborers here in 1868. They used â€˜squatter's rights' to build a ramshackle colony along the Truckee River on what is now River Avenue East. A huge fire destroyed Chinatown on May 29, 1875, but the Orientals rebuilt their community. Finally, on Nov. 18, 1878, a group of about 500 citizens, under the leadership of the Caucasian League, tore down the shanty town and ordered the 1000 or more Chinese to leave. They simply moved across the river, adjoining this former site of a Chinese herb shop, and built anew. A third fire, on Jan. 3, 1883, consumed the 'new' Chinatown and brought about the gradual end to the Chinese colony here. Bronze plaque dedicated 1980 by Chap 3691 E Clampus Vitus. Text of bronze plaque: Old Chinese Herb Shop.
address: Corner of S.E. River St. and Brockway Rd.

image.alt: Old Chinese Herb Shop, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Old North San Juan School
description: The first school building on this site was moved from Flume Street in 1869. It burned in 1924, and this building was moved here from Lake Vera. This site operated as a school from 1869 until 1984. It is currently used as a community center and library.
address: 29190 Hwy 49, south end of North San Juan

name: Old Pacific Fruit Packing Shed
description: A smaller shed, built here in 1926, collapsed during a snowstorm in 1929. Its successor was built in 1930 by the Civilian Conservation Corps. The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad tracks, depot, and water tower were alongside this building, where peaches and pears from the surrounding orchards were packed and sent to Colfax by train. There the boxes were transferred to standard sized refrigerator freight cars to be shipped to the East.
address: Mount Olive and Lower Colfax Rds, Chicago Park

name: Old Stone Garage
description: Truckee's Old Stone Garage and Site of Town's First Dwelling. In 1863, Joseph Gray built Truckee's first structure on this site for use as the Dutch Flat-Donner Lake Wagon Road's toll station. The old log structure was moved in 1907 to the Englehart property on Church Street, one block east of this site, where it still stands and the Brethren of ECV dedicated a plaque in 1965. In 1909, D. J. Smith, with the help of European-trained stone masons, constructed the present building for use as a livery and garage.
address: Southwest corner Jibboom and Bridge Sts

image.alt: Old Stone Garage, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Original Ott Assay Office Site
description: Site of the Original Ott Assay Office. J. J. Ott's assay in 1859 helped develop the great Comstock Lode and the State of Nevada. Note: Ott's original Assay Office was built of wood. It burned in the fire of 1863 and Ott moved into the existing brick building next door.

name: Ormonde Town Site
description: The town of Ormonde was established here in 1887. Downstream, across the mouth of Canyon Creek was Canyonville 1850, and upriver approximately four miles was Maybert 1886. These towns were part of the upper Washington Mining District and were the furthest upstream of the serious mining ventures along the South Yuba. During its brief history the district yielded 17 million dollars in gold, but by 1897 it was nearly totally abandoned. The remains have since been obliterated by flooding. Note: The town consisted of 8-10 buildings, including a store, saloon and boarding house.
address: Maybert Rd, 3.6 m. east of Washington. About 3/10 m. past Maybert (Canyon Creek) bridge in Golden Quartz campground. Maybert Rd does not require 4-wheel-drive but not advised for cars with low clearance.

image.alt: Ormonde Town Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Overland Emigrant Trail--Highway 49
description: At this point, the old Overland Emigrant Trail approaches the present highway. More than 100-years ago it resounded to the creaking wheels of pioneer wagons and the cry of hardy but footsore travelers buoyed by the realization that their long trip to the goldfields was about over.
address: Hwy 49 south of Grass Valley, 1.2 m. north of Combie Rd between Clivus Dr and Brewer Rd at South Wolf Creek bridge

image.alt: Overland Emigrant Trail--Highway 49, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Pelton Water Wheel
description: No. 4, 1928-1987. Produced 18,000 HP at 257 RPM and enough electrical power for 16,000 households. 12 ft. diameter, weight 15 tons. Built in San Francisco by Pelton Company. A Constitutional Bicentennial gift to Nevada City by Pacific Gas and Electric.

image.alt: Pelton Water Wheel, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Pollard's Station
description: On this site, Joseph Delos Pollard constructed the first resort on the west end of Donner Lake. Between mid 1864 and early 1868 Pollard's Station on the Dutch Flat & Donner Lake Wagon Road was office to the Pioneer Stage Co. and Wells, Fargo & Co. Express, as well as a hotel, boasting bar room, reading room, billiards and a bowling alley. Visitors could rent boats, horses or carriages to enjoy the scenic grandeur. The station was ravaged by fire twice and rebuilt once. The station was no longer needed due to the completion of the railroad in June 1868, which put a virtual end to wagon and stage traffic across the Sierra.
address: Near intersection of S. Shore Dr and Donner Pass Rd, west of Truckee

image.alt: Pollard's Station, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Lawrence & Houseworth, publisher, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Purdon Crossing Bridge
description: Purdon Bridge 1895. Built by Cotton Brothers of Oakland, this steel bridge replaced several wooden bridges washed away in floods. It is the only bridge of the half-through truss design remaining in California. Purdon Crossing was a vital link of the main road from Nevada City to Downieville and the Northern Mines. Note: The unique quality of a half-through truss is that the deck is carried halfway between the top and bottom of the support structure; very few were built in California. First bridge at this site was built by William E. Robinson in 1854. In 1859 John Webber dismantled Robinson's Bridge and replaced it. Webber's Bridge washed away in December 1861 and was replaced by a rope suspension foot bridge in February 1862. Jesse Wall bought the foot bridge and crossing in the spring of 1862 and built a new bridge that summer. Wall sold it to James M. Black in 1864. Peter Purdon bought the bridge in 1870 and sold it to the county in 1890, which replaced it with this one in 1895.
address: Bottom of Purdon Rd at South Fork of Yuba River, north of Nevada City

image.alt: Purdon Crossing Bridge, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Jet Lowe Related names: California Department of Transportation Yearby, Jean P, transmitter, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Railroad and Transportation Museum
description: The Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad (1876_1942) ran 22 miles from Nevada City through Grass Valley, its headquarters; to Colfax, Placer County, where it met the transcontinental line of the Central (later Southern) Pacific Railroad. Built by John F. Kidder, it was the main freight and passenger route serving the western county. Kidder ran the railroad until his death in 1901 and was followed by his widow Sarah, who became the first woman railroad president in United States. She served until 1913. This museum is dedicated to preserving the NCNGRR's place in history and to those who built, operated and maintained the little road. Second plaque dedicated 2011 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Railroad and Transportation Museum. The Nevada County Historical Society founded the Transportation Museum Division in 1983 to acquire, document, restore and preserve information and artifacts pertaining to Nevada County's railroad and transportation history. Best known is the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad that operated from 1876 until 1942. The museum opened for public enjoyment on March 17, 2003.
address: Five Kidder Court, Nevada City. Take Ridge Rd exit off Hwy 49

image.alt: Railroad and Transportation Museum, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Red Dog Town Site
description: Note: The site is a federally restricted area. Red Dog (1851-1871), with a population of 2,000 at its peak, was the third town of importance in Nevada County, surviving longer than most of the earliest gold towns. The Post Office closed in 1869.
address: Red Dog Rd, 3 m. north of You Bet Rd intersection. 0.5 m. southeast of Greenhorn Creek

image.alt: Red Dog Town Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Relief Hill
description: Note: Access to the old townsite and historic cemetery is sometimes restricted by the US Forest Service. Description: Relief Hill dates from 1853. By 1856, it had a population of 75, two saloons, one store, one butcher shop, one blacksmith shop, two boarding houses and several homes. In 1858, registered voters alone numbered 100. Its decline followed the Sawyer decision enjoining certain aspects of hydraulic mining. In the early 1900s, some residents moved closer to Relief Hill Road. Some of those buildings can still be seen. While there is still some intermittent mining in the area, by the 1960s, the old townsite was largely deserted. Not much but the cemetery remains.
address: South of intersection of Relief Hill Rd and Relief Hill Mine Rd, about 5 m. east of North Bloomfield

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Relief Hill
image.originalAlt: Relief Hill in

name: Robinson Plaza
description: Robinson Plaza named for Beryl Robinson Jr. Born in Nevada City September 13, 1935. City Councilman-City Manager. On July 1,1999, Beryl Robinson Jr. became the longest-serving City Manager in state history. A native of the town he has so ably served, Beryl was appointed City Manager on June 21, 1965. In addition to being Nevada City's chief administrative and financial official, he has diligently worked to preserve the community's 19th-century spirit and buildings, including the two historic structures here at Robinson Plaza. Beryl's continuing career in public service has already spanned the terms of seven presidents, six governors and three-dozen council members. A Mason, Rotarian and Elk, Beryl has brought a sense of commitment and purpose two his life matched by few and bettered by none.
address: Foot of Main St, Nevada City. The plaza is dedicated to Beryl Robinson and contains several sites and plaques.

image.alt: Robinson Plaza, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Rocking Stone
description: A natural glacial formation, or the work of an unknown tribe as a form of altar. Its exact origin will never be known. The perfectly balanced stone until recently would rock at the touch of a finger. C. F. McGlashan built the original tower in 1895 and used it to display Donner Party relics and his famous butterfly collection. Site of an overnight stop for the Olympic Torch in 1960.
address: Between High St and Keiser Ave, near Spring St

image.alt: Rocking Stone, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: National Geodetic Survey NGS. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), National Ocean Service (NOS) office., via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Rollins Dam and Reservoir
description: Two plaques dedicated 1966 by Nevada Irrigation District: "Nevada Irrigation District, Yuba-Bear River Development, Rollins Dam & Reservoir Completed 1965.â€
address: Larsen Rd about 1000 ft from Orchard Springs access road to Rollins Lake, Chicago Park

image.alt: Rollins Dam and Reservoir, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Root Cellar on Mexican War Land Grant
description: This old root cellar is located on property that originally was part of a Land Grant of 160 acres granted to Josiah Dodge by President Ulysses Grant in 1873 as Bounty Land for his service as a private with Captain Steen's Co., 1st U.S. Dragoons, War with Mexico. In 1878 this property became part of the John Bree Ranch. The root cellar, built by Bree in the 1880s, was used to store his produce. Mr. Bree was among the first in the county to plant a Bartlett Pear orchard for commercial purposes.
address: 16330 Gibboney Lane, Grass Valley. Take Hwy 49 to Alta Sierra intersection and follow Alta Sierra Dr to Gibboney Lane

image.alt: Root Cellar on Mexican War Land Grant, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Rough and Ready Cemetery
description: Established in 1850, it has been in continuous use, and contains approximately 400 burials. The site includes an unusual amount of well-preserved grave monuments and cast-iron fencing. The town was named by its founder, Captain A.A. Townshend, in honor of General Zachery Taylor, “Old Rough and Ready,” the 12th President of the United States.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Rough and Ready Cemetery

name: Rough and Ready Schoolhouse Site
description: Note: Present owner requests privacy. Landmark: The original one-room Rough and Ready school was built in 1868. It served the community for many generations before being destroyed in the Trauner Fire of 1994. The house Kevin and Gaylene Collins built here in 1995 has an exterior like that of the schoolhouse.
address: 10079 Blue Creek Rd, south side of Old Rough and Ready Hwy, opposite Cemetery entrance

image.alt: Rough and Ready Schoolhouse Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Rough and Ready Toll House
description: This house and the adjacent property contain five plaques. First plaque: Built in 1859 by Benoni Thompson to collect tolls for using the Gephardt Union Turnpike running 6 miles from Penn Valley to Grass Valley. The last remaining original toll house in the West. It served as a Wells Fargo Express office and stage stop, and was the secret rendezvous place of Joaquin Murietta and Lola Montez. [Note: The reference to Joaquin Murietta and Lola Montez must be taken with a grain of salt and credited to the droll humor of E Clampus Vitus. There is no evidence of such a friendship, although anything was possible in the Old West.]

image.alt: Rough and Ready Toll House, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Mark Twain, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Rough and Ready Town Site
description: Rough and Ready. Established In 1849, and named after the Rough and Ready Company of miners from Wisconsin, in honor of General Zachary Taylor, this was one of the principal towns of Nevada County. Here in 1850, articles of secession were drawn up establishing the 'Republic of Rough and Ready.' As a result of disastrous fires, only a few structures remain today of those built in the 1850s.
address: Rough and Ready Hwy, corner Chapel Rd, Rough and Ready

image.alt: Rough and Ready Town Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Rowe Saw Mill
description: Fourth plaque: Rowe Saw Mill. In the 1860s there were four saw mills in this area. This display, in honor of his founding fathers, is from the Tom Rowe Saw Mill, and displays how the mill works. The foreman stood in the cage and called out the beam size (8x8, 12x12, etc.) then the cutter set the blade which was on a track, and rode the blade down the track until the beam cut was complete. The beam then went to the mines to build the shafts.
address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready

image.alt: Rowe Saw Mill, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Saint Columncille's Catholic Church
description: The original plaque disappeared and was replaced in 1949 by Native Daughters of the Golden West by a wooden plaque: St. Columncille's Catholic Church. Formerly the Bridgeport Union Guard Hall, was used in 1860 to train men for the Civil War under Capt. Frank Coffey of French Corral. In 1880, it was purchased as a house of worship for the Catholics of the ridge towns. Note: This building formerly was located at Birchville. In 1969 it was purchased by Babe Pinaglia and given to Malakoff Diggins Park, where it occupies the site of the Catholic Church that once served the miners of North Bloomfield.
address: No. Bloomfield Rd, west of North Bloomfield, Malakoff Diggins State Park

image.alt: Saint Columncille's Catholic Church, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Saint Paul Lutheran Church Site
description: On this site donated by Anna Pusheck the German Evangelical Lutheran St. Paul congregation was established in 1902 for worship and praise to God, continuing until 1984. Note: Anna Pusheck was the mother of Dr. Charles Pusheck, one of the Chicago Park Colony's developers. In 1895, he built a sanitorium in Chicago Park, which he later converted to a hotel. The St. Paul Lutheran church building was dedicated in December 1904. After the church was destroyed by fire on MotherÃ
address: St. Paul Pl. east of Hwy 174 at Chicago Park. St. Paul Cemetery is first left past the fire station

image.alt: Saint Paul Lutheran Church Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Schallenberger Cabin Site
description: Near this spot stood a small cabin built by 18-year old Moses Schallenberger and two other men. They were members of the Stephens-Townsend-Murphy party of 1844, the first pioneers to take wagons over the Sierra Nevada, opening the Truckee Route of the California Trail. The three men had volunteered to remain behind and guard six of the wagons left here by the main party. Due to extreme winter weather conditions and lack of food, the three men agreed to separate. The two older men rejoined the wagon party, but due to his weakened physical condition, Moses Schallenberger chose to remain at the cabin. The young man survived for nearly three months alone in the small cabin during the winter of 1844-45 before being rescued. His cabin would later provide shelter for the Breen family, members of the ill-fated Donner Party, who were stranded here in 1846-47. The courage and resolution of Moses Schallenberger during his solitary winter ordeal in this cabin makes him one of the true heroes in the saga of the California Trail. This monument was made possible through the generosity of many groups and individuals committed to the preservation of the California Trail. Note: The monument contains a time capsule to be opened on August 19, 2095.
address: In Donner Memorial State Park, about 75 yds. east of Museum

image.alt: Schallenberger Cabin Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Sheriff David Fulton Douglass
description: On this spot Sheriff Douglass, a Native Son of the Golden West, gave his life July 26, 1896, bravely performing his duty. Alone he tracked a highwayman to this retreat and both fell In battle. It is believed Douglass was pitted against two and that one escaped. The bodies lay parallel. Nearby is a second plaque identifying the spot where the bandit lay.
address: Just past 15934 Airport Rd, 3/4 m. from junction with Lake Vera Rd, Nevada City

image.alt: Sheriff David Fulton Douglass, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Shoot (Chute) Hill
description: Local historians relate that water from high mountain lakes flowed thru a 500' wooden 'chute' which led to a huge square tank or pressure box behind campsite 29. Here it entered a 3' pipe reduced to gain pressure to 2', then down hill 200' to the hydraulic monitors at the mine.
address: Across from space 29 in Chute Hill campground about 7/10 m. east of North Bloomfield

image.alt: Shoot (Chute) Hill, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Sierra Mountain Cemetery
description: The Masonic and Odd Fellows lodges each established cemeteries here in 1869 or 1870 to provide a burial place for their deceased. Catholics established a cemetery to the southwest. Pioneers and prominent citizens were buried within the original cemetery grounds. The less reputable were buried outside the fences. Stories abound of the bodies placed in snowbanks or ice houses awaiting the ground thaw. The Truckee Cemetery District was formed in 1946 and received title to the land and named the cemetery in 1964. In 1954 the original site increased in size despite the best efforts of the community to prevent the townfolk from dying.
address: East end of E. Jibboom St

image.alt: Sierra Mountain Cemetery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Sierra Sun (Truckee Republican)
description: Oldest continuous business enterprise in the Central Sierra commenced publication in 1869 as the 'Truckee Republican' and was also informally known as the 'Truckee Screwdriver.' The definitive 'History of the Donner Party' was first published in this newspaper in 1879 when author, teacher, statesman C. F. McGlashan was its editor. Note: The Truckee Republican, which began life as the Grass Valley Republican, commenced publishing at Truckee in 1872, not 1869. Its descendant is now the Sierra Sun. The plaque was moved in 2014 from 12315 Deerfield Drive, and cannot currently be located.

image.alt: Sierra Sun (Truckee Republican), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Snow Tent Site
description: Founded in 1850, Snow Tent flourished in the second half of the 19th century as a center for mining and lumbering and as an important stop on one of the routes through the Henness Pass. Snow Tent's best-known institution was its hotel, sometimes known as Snow Tent House. Snow Tent lies in a comparatively uninhabited part of the Tahoe National Forest, and there are no readily visible historic remains of the community.
address: Near the intersection of Snow Tent and N. Bloomfield-Graniteville Rds.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Snow Tent Site

name: South Yuba Canal Office
description: Headquarters for the largest network of water flumes and ditches In the state. The South Yuba Canal Water Company was the first incorporated to supply water for hydraulic mining. The original ditch was in use in May, 1850, and this company office was in use from 1857 to 1880. The holdings later became the vast P.G. & E. hydro-electric system.Second plaque dedicated 1987 by Native Sons of the Golden West: US Constitution 1787 Bicentennial 1987 Nevada City, a Bicentennial Community. The plaques are mounted on adjacent brick buildings. The one on the left originally was the Potter Building and the other began life as the City Drugstore, Both survived the great fire of 1856 and are two of the oldest in Nevada City. The South Yuba Canal Water Company did not occupy the building until 1861.

image.alt: South Yuba Canal Office, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Stampede Circle of Stones
description: The Stampede Circle of Stones is the oldest manmade structure in the Truckee region-at least 500 years old. Built by the Washoe or their predecessors, the Martis people, the Circle's original home was in Stampede Valley, about 10 miles northeast of here. In 1969 it was moved to this location shortly before the construction of the Stampede Dam flooded the valley. Archaeological digging on the original site indicates it may have been used as an antelope corral.
address: 10600 Brockway Rd, northeast corner of Truckee River Regional Park, Truckee

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Stampede Circle of Stones
image.originalAlt: Stampede Circle of Stones in

name: Summit City/Meadow Lake
description: In June of 1863, the trapper Henry Hartley discovered gold bearing ore here and staked out the Excelsior Company claims. Exaggerated reports and a 1865 market panic in the Comstock mines of Virginia City brought thousands of miners overnight to the supposed 'Little Comstock' of Summit City. Within two years an incorporated town, renamed Meadow Lake grew to encompass 160 acres with 4,000 summer residents and over 500 buildings, including 200 business houses, a stock exchange, daily newspaper, Post Office, school, 6 breweries and 80 saloons. Streets were laid out grid fashion, with a 9,000 square foot plaza on the lakeshore. An excursion vessel ferried revelers to four hurdy-gurdy houses at the lake's upper end. The winter of 1866-1867 brought 30 feet of snow with the 'Snowshoe Express' delivering mail through Cisco. In all the eight stamp mills in a district of 10,910 claims failed to extract the expected gold from its 'Rebellious Ore.' The optimism faded and the town declined with like rapidity. July of 1869 found 60 residents. By 1873 only Hartley 'The Hermit' remained. His grave is on a hillside to the west of town. A wooden sign nearby was erected at an unknown date by Troop 40, Kentfield-Marin Council, Boy Scouts of America: Meadow Lake-Excelsior. The granite dam was built 1858 for flumes of valley gold towns. Called Summit City with 2000 pop. after 1866. It incorporated as Meadow Lake 1866. Because gold could not be separated it was abandoned 1872.
address: Meadow Lake Rd, south of Jackson Meadows Rd

image.alt: Summit City/Meadow Lake, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: The Tin Can -- Dot's
description: The two modern buildings replaced structures built in the early 20th-century and used in two of Truckee's notorious industries: bootlegging and prostitution. They upheld a tradition begun on this street in the 1870's. Many a thirst was slaked and need fulfilled because of these buildings' contents. Politely ignored, although they never failed because of lack of business, they operated until mid-century to serve the working men of this and surrounding communities. Changing commercial interests and ultimately property values doomed these buildings to history.

image.alt: The Tin Can -- Dot's, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: The Tin Lantern Bar and Grill, via Google

name: Theodore Dehone Judah
description: As Chief Engineer of the Central Pacific Railroad, Judah's engineering skill, political savvy and financial genius, along with the promoters' capital, culminated in the formation and construction of the Trans-Sierra leg of the first transcontinental railroad. While fighting the excesses of the railroadÃ
address: Track side of Amtrak station, 10065 Donner Pass Rd

image.alt: Theodore Dehone Judah, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Toll Road Stake
description: Second Plaque: Toll Road Stake. Across the street from this location is a tree stump in which the original stake exists that was used to close off the passage to travelers from Grass Valley to Penn Valley. Drivers who frequented this road had to stop at this location and pay the toll. Once appropriate compensation was provided, the gate would be opened to allow passage.
address: 14365 Old Rough and Ready Hwy, Rough and Ready

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Toll Road Stake
image.originalAlt: Toll Road Stake in

name: Town of Washington
description: Washington, originally Indiana Boys Camp, is a town that refused to ghost. It was settled in 1849. By 1880 miners had recovered over $10,500,000 in gold here. Note: First plaque is on the Washington Hotel. Note: The only mining town in the county not destroyed by fire. Today, Washington (pop. about 180) is one of the few remaining early mining towns. The permanent population swells to hundreds more residents in the summer months. A second plaque, shown above, was dedicated at an unknown date in a small park at the intersection of Washington Rd and Alpha Toll Rd: In August, 1850 Washington was the highest point on the South Yuba River at which gold had been discovered, with over a 1,000 miners. In 1870 the Chinese outnumbered the white population. The last try to strike it rich was in the 1890's. Washington is the last remaining village of the many that flourished in the Washington Mining District. Historical notes by Robert I. and Grace I. Slyter.
address: Bottom of Washington Rd on South Fork of the Yuba River, 18 m. northeast of Nevada City via Hwy 20

image.alt: Town of Washington, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Trans-Sierra Freeway Project
description: Plaque dedicated 1966 by the American Society of Civil Engineering: Civil Engineering Achievement of Merit, 1966, Awarded by the American Society of Civil Engineers.
address: Highway rest stops on both sides of I-80, at Donner Summit, west of Truckee

image.alt: Trans-Sierra Freeway Project, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Truckee Flag Pole
description: Truckee / Jewel of the Eastern Sierras. First known as Gray's Toll Station, then Coburn's Station, Truckee was established in 1863. Renowned for its lumber industry, transcontinental railroad, icehouses, saloons and red light district, Truckee has never been known for her laid back demeanor. It has always been a town where people came to entertain themselves on Friday and Saturday nights, just as it is today. Second plaque dedicated 1994 by Truckee Fire Protection District: Truckee Fire Protection District. This flagpole represents 100 years of service by the Truckee Fire Protection District and its members, past and present for their commitment to the preservation of life and property.
address: 10065 Donner Pass Rd, northwest side of Amtrak station

image.alt: Truckee Flag Pole, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Truckee Jail
description: 1875 Old Truckee Jail. Built in September 1875 and in continuous use until 1964. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1971 by Nevada County, NEV 04: The first extension of the Nevada County jail which was built in 1875 by order of the County Supervisors on the petition of residents in Meadow Lake and Truckee townships due to the high cost of transporting prisoners from the eastern part of the county to the jail in Nevada City. Built of stone at a cost of $2,500 the building housed its first prisoner in September 1875. Note: Building now houses the Truckee-Donner Historical Museum.
address: Northeast corner of Spring and Jibboom Sts

image.alt: Truckee Jail, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Truckee Round House Site
description: Needed to house and maintain the engines of the Central Pacific Railroad during construction of the trans-Sierra crossing, an enclosed wooden round house was constructed here in 1868. It was razed by fire in March of 1869. A 16-stall wooden structure was built that year as the maintenance facility of the C.P.R.R., Truckee Division. Another fire in 1876 resulted in construction of a 22-stall granite and wooden structure. This served through 1942, when it was officially retired, a victim of the end of the steam era and consolidation of maintenance at other locations. The round houses were home to helper engines, fire trains and the first rotary snow plow in the Sierra.
address: East end of municipal parking lot, just west of 10021 Donner Pass Rd

image.alt: Truckee Round House Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Truckee's First Log Cabin
description: First Log Cabin. Erected by Joseph Gray at S.W. corner of present day Bridge and Spring streets to serve Dutch Flat Wagon Road traffic over Donner Pass. Removed to this site 1907.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Truckee's First Log Cabin
image.originalAlt: Truckee's First Log Cabin in

name: Truckee's Vigilance ("601") Committee
description: Truckee's vigilance committee, known as the 601, was born in response to the rise in crime and 'undesirables' which plagued the young railroad town. In late 1874 and early 1875, the 601 carried out a campaign to intimidate and rid the town of those elements. After accidentally shooting one of their own members, the 601 disbanded until August 1889 when they reassembled for one last time. Though their goals were never fully achieved, the 601 did bring a semblance of law and order to the area through their vigilance.
address: On sidewalk in front of 10096 Donner Pass Rd, across street from Amtrak Station,

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Truckee's Vigilance ("601") Committee
image.originalAlt: Truckee's Vigilance ("601") Committee in

name: Uncle Joe's Cabin
description: Joseph Gray believed that this area would be a good place for a tavern and way station for weary travelers. It was near this spot that transportation routes converged—the Henness Pass route from the upper North Fork of the Yuba, and the Dutch Flat-Donner Lake turnpike. Five years hence the Central Pacific would choose the same area as the focal point for its mountain operations. Constructed of native tamarack and lodgepole pine in about 1858, Gray’s cabin was located at today’s southwest corner of Jibboom and Bridge Streets. Experts have examined the 24’x20’ building and noticed no evidence of any kind of saw used to build the structure. The timbers had all been hewn with axes into the shape of a house.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Uncle Joe's Cabin

name: Union Hill
description: Union Hill School was established in March 1868 as a one-room schoolhouse on the grounds of Union Hill Mine. On June 26, 1875, an argument over a trustee election took place at the schoolhouse, resulting in a stabbing and subsequent death. Three days later an arsonist burned down the schoolhouse. In the late 1870s, a new schoolhouse was built on the current location and was in service until 1940. The modern building has been in continuous use since 1940. Union Hill has proudly served our community for over 144 years.
address: 10879 Bartlett Dr, just off Hwy 174, Grass Valley CA

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Union Hill

name: Veterans Memorial Flagstaffs, Grass Valley
description: Plaques and flagstaffs dedicated 1954 by Post 130, American Legion Auxiliary: Lest We Forget. This memorial flagstaff dedicated in eternal memory of those war veterans resting within the portals of this cemetery who served in the wars of our nation and those who gave their lives defending AmericaÃ
address: Entrances to Greenwood and Catholic cemeteries, W. Main St, Grass Valley

image.alt: Veterans Memorial Flagstaffs, Grass Valley, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Victory Highway Monument
description: First plaque dedicated 1928. Text of plaque: Dedicated to California's sons and daughters who served their country in the World War 1917-1918 and to the memory of those who gave the last full measure of devotion. Second plaque and monument dedicated 1998 by Truckee Town Council and California Department of Transportation: Victory Highway Monument Rededication July 24, 1998. This monument was originally sited on old Highway 40 near the California/Nevada line in 1928 and removed in the mid 1970s due to vandalism. It was one of a series of monuments placed on the 'Victory Highway' across the U.S., honoring those who died serving the United States in World War I. Donner Pass Road and part of Glenshire Drive through Truckee still follow the basic alignment of the old highway. The Town of Truckee, in cooperation with CalTrans, has rededicated this monument not only for its original purpose and the history of Old Highway 40, but also for current revitalization efforts occurring in historic downtown Truckee.
address: Northwest corner of Spring St and Donner Pass Rd, Truckee

image.alt: Victory Highway Monument, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: E bailey, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 4.0)

name: Washington Elementary School
description: 100 Years of uninterrupted use as a California public school. Having survived fires, floods, wars, time, and politics, this school has been doing its part to ensure the rights of American children to receive a basic education.

image.alt: Washington Elementary School, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Washington Hotel and Site
description: Hessel Buisman built the first hotel on this site in 1857. It was destroyed by fire in August 1867, along with neighboring businesses. Buisman replaced it with a two-story hotel in that same year. In 1887, Buisman's daughter Harminia and her husband, E. T. Worthley, inherited the hotel, which burned in April 1896. The Worthleys reopened it with a third story in August 1896. John J. Rogers bought the hotel in 1910 for $50, and in 1911 he installed a water pipeline across the river to the Nice and Latta ditch to furnish water for the town. In 1912, he used the water to generate the first electricity for the hotel. Thomas B. Williamson, who had purchased a half interest in the hotel for $10 in 1911, bought out Rogers in 1916, and then sold it to Pietro Orzalli. Orzalli sold the property to R. F. Williamson (brother of Tom) in 1925. In 1926, Emory J. Haverstock bought the hotel and added dormer windows on the top floor, using lumber salvaged from the fire-ravaged Dugan Hotel (also in Washington). Haverstock sold it to Clark and Edna Waite for $10 in 1939. In 1955, the hotel was sold for $10 to Harold Moneyhun, former owner of the Holbrooke Hotel in Grass Valley, and Edwin Furano of Nevada City. In 1968, Thomas E. Walsh used siding salvaged from the National Hotel Annex in Nevada City to enclose the side porch on the upriver side of the hotel.

image.alt: Washington Hotel and Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Washoe Tribe Grinding Stone-Lam
description: Plaque placed 2017: Washoe Tribe Grinding Stone-Lam. The Washoe Tribe are the original inhabitants of the Truckee-Tahoe region. The Washoe people utilized bed rock mortar for food processing, they call it a Lam. Much of the Washoe heritage resources have been lost due to development. This Lam is a casualty of development, displaced from its original location and is an example of past practices. Working together, the Town of Truckee, Mountain Area Preservation and the Washoe Tribe agreed to place the Lam at the Trout Creek Pocket Park in order to keep it close to its original location and as a reminder of the original people who lived in the region.
address: Northeast corner of Jibboom and Bridge Sts

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Washoe Tribe Grinding Stone-Lam

name: Wells Fargo Building Site Nevada City
description: Site of Old Wells Fargo Building 1853. Dedicated to the Pioneers of California by Hydraulic Parlor No. 56 N.S.G.W. July 5, 1926. Plaque erected by Wells Fargo Bank and Union Trust Co. S.F. Calif.

image.alt: Wells Fargo Building Site Nevada City, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Wells Fargo Building, North San Juan
description: Wells Fargo & Company 1854. Office and holding center for gold and currency, 1854-60. Block & Furth Co. Clothing Store, 1861-69. Chinese Laundry and store operated by Happy Go Lucky, 1890-1920. Bar and liquor store since 1934. First saloon owner, â€˜Ma' Cunningham, 1934.
address: Corner of Flume St and Hwy 49

image.alt: Wells Fargo Building, North San Juan, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Western America Skisport Museum
description: A monument and statue was placed by Snowshoe Thompson Lodge No. 78, Sons of Norway, Yuba City: Snowshoe Thompson. A hero of the Sierra Nevada mountains! Probably the first skier of the West. This Viking Son of Norway exemplified the spirit of a true pioneer: Strong, daring, faithful and courageous. He was the answer to CaliforniaÃ
address: Exit on Boreal Ridge Rd on I-80 west of summit

name: World War I Memorial, Grass Valley
description: Plaque dedicated 1921: Honor Roll. Heroes from the Grass Valley District who gave their lives for their country 1917-1918: Anthony J. Bevilacqua, Robert H. Beyer, Lawrence A. Brasher, Melvyn Frandy, Isaac Leslie Fleming, Hedley Hill, Lenville Dooley Jatunn, William Hague, William A. Kneebone, Albert Pinkham, William Hawley Moran, James Rodgers, Clarence Crase Thomas, J. M. Spencer, Elton Thomas Whiting, John Edward White. Recently, a small plaque at the base of the main plaque added U.S. Army Nurse Elina W. Hill to the Honor Roll. Note: The above inscription is on a very large monument. Surrounding it is a grove of 16 trees, each with a marker in front inscribed with the name of one of these men and the American Legion logotype.
address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley

image.alt: World War I Memorial, Grass Valley, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: World War II & Korean War Memorial
description: Plaque dedicated by Grass Valley Elks No. 538 and Frank Gallino Post No. 130 American Legion: World War II 1941-1946 Korean 1950-1955. To the men and women who made the supreme sacrifice for our country and her noble purpose, in memory of those who died that liberty might live. Note: Plaque is on a concrete monument that includes a flagpole and benches.
address: Memorial Park, corner of Race St and Colfax Ave, Grass Valley

image.alt: World War II & Korean War Memorial, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: You Bet Town Site
description: This mining camp, established between 1858-1860, consisted of 40 or 50 buildings by 1864. A school house was built midway between You Bet and Red Dog. The town was destroyed by fire in April 1869, partially rebuilt, and burned again in September 1873 and August 1915. The courts halted most hydraulic mining in 1884, but gold mining continued until the 1940s. The You Bet Post Office was closed in 1903.
address: Red Dog Rd, between Lowell Hill and Chalk Bluff Rds, six m. east of You Bet Rd exit on Hwy 174

image.alt: You Bet Town Site, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: "Path" on Brockway Road by Artelettica
description: Artists: Arteclettica - an artistic partnership between Dominic Panziera & Daniela Garofalo, presents "Path," a tile and metal sculpture with vibrant colors and wandering patterns that remind us of the people and creatures that continue to pass through the Sierra Nevada.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for "Path" on Brockway Road by Artelettica

name: Animal Tracks"" -- bike rack project. At Tahoe Forest Hospital.
description: Artist: Kevin Kremler. ''Animal Tracks'' is a painted steel bike rack sculpture featuring paw prints of local animals. It was made by artist Kevin Kremler and is part of the TPAC Truckee Artistic Bike Rack Project. It was commissioned by Jae Gustafson for Tahoe Forest Hospital.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Animal Tracks"" -- bike rack project. At Tahoe Forest Hospital.

name: Aspen Grove"" in Radiation Lobby at Tahoe Forest Cancer Center
description: Artist: Troy Corliss. The colorful array of glass leaves in the Troy Corliss думAspen Groveдуќ welcomes patients and visitors to the Radiation Therapy waiting space. Fabricated in steel and kiln fused glass, the artwork integrates an experience of the natural world with interior architecture. The gentle curves of the tree trunks and the subtle patterning and warm colors of the glass leaves creates a three dimensional design that is both intriguing and calming. The Grove can be experienced as a powerful whole at first sight or explored with interest with repeated visits.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Aspen Grove"" in Radiation Lobby at Tahoe Forest Cancer Center

name: Community As Ecosystem""
description: Artists: Sara L. Smith, Toni Standteiner/Mountain Forge. Commissioned by the TRDRPD, 'думCommunity As Ecosystem" main mural image measures more than 11 ft. high by 16 ft. wide. The piece celebrates Truckee's new Aquatic Center donors. The artists represent the concept of joyful balance noting each donor (no matter level of contribution) is a vital part of the overall effort. The mural speaks to the wider concept of community as an ecosystem, and reminds us that we are all essential. The artists note: "The mural is painted using sepia tones to tie us back to our past, and is painted upon a wall created with reclaimed Lake Tahoe pier wood, creating a tangible tie to our physical past and present. It depicts a stylized image of Martis Valleyду»s diverse natural environment, and donors are represented by a combination of cut and manipulated animal shapes and forged iron environmental elements, such as mountain peaks, cattails and wave forms in the creek. On a separate panel each and every donorду»s name has been hand lettered, grouped next to its corresponding donor category and animal shape.дуќ In addition two additional small panels were created to specifically recognize the individuals who helped shepherd the project to fruition, and well as the organizations that contributed multi-million dollar grant funds.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Community As Ecosystem""

name: DeRailed"" bike rack
description: Artist: Bernie LaForest of Striker Forge. ''DeRailed'' which is part of the Truckee Artistic Bike Rack Project by TPAC, is made of repurposed rail steel. Created by artist Bernie LaForest of Striker Forge, it represents the railRd. and its role bringing commerce to California. It is fittingly placed in the plaza across from Truckee Train Station and was commissioned by the Town of Truckee.

name: Locomotion"" bike sculpture
description: Artist: Fred Besch. ''Locomotion'' was created by local kinetic artist Fred Besch. The giant bike sculpture can be found in the plaza west of the historic Flying A building. name: Martis Sculpture"" - front of Martis Outlook professional building.
description: Artist: Anton Standteiner, Mountain Forge. ''Martis Sculpture'' was created by artist Anton Standteiner of The Mountain Forge and commissioned by the Town of Truckee.The flow and style of the sculpture honors the Native American people who once inhabited Martis Valley. It stands in front of the Martis Outlook professional building on the corner of Soaring Way and Truckee Tahoe Airport Way.
